serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of oyster omelette as served at a hawker centre or seafood restaurant in Singapore, approximately 250 g, comprising fried oysters, egg, and starch pancake with chilli sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Oyster Omelette (Orh Luak) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Health Promotion Board Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists oyster omelette (orh luak) as a hawker dish. Per-100 g values are derived from the HPB nutrient composition data for this dish. The restaurant version (The Seafood Market) may use higher-quality oysters and more oil, so values represent a standard hawker-style preparation. Where HPB data gaps exist for micronutrients, USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) values for comparable egg-and-shellfish dishes were used as secondary reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; analogous estimate based on starch-thickened egg and shellfish pancake
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for oyster omelette. GI estimated at 52 based on the dominant glycemic driver being sweet potato starch (GI ~44–54 per University of Sydney GI Database for sweet potato) combined with egg and oyster components which contribute negligible glycemic load. The starch-thickened pancake matrix is analogous to a sweet-potato-starch-based dish. GI 52 is a conservative mid-range estimate within the low category (≤55). GL per serving = (52 × 11.8 × 250/100) / 100 = 15.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~950 mg per serving) from fish sauce, soy sauce, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit oyster omelette to occasional consumption and request reduced fish sauce and seasoning, as a standard 250 g serving provides approximately 950 mg sodium, representing nearly 40% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (GL ~15 per serving) driven by sweet potato starch batter; moderate carbohydrate content of 11.8 g per 100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can consume oyster omelette in moderate portions, as the low GI (estimated 52) and protein-fat matrix slow glucose absorption, but the starch batter contributes a moderate glycemic load of approximately 15 per standard serving and portion control is advised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated cholesterol (~185 mg per 100 g from eggs and oysters), saturated fat (2.8 g per 100 g), and high sodium; oysters provide beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and zinc
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume oyster omelette in moderation due to its cholesterol and saturated fat content from eggs and frying oil, and its high sodium level, though the oyster component provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids and zinc that partially offset these concerns.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oyster omelette (蚝煎蛋, also known as orh luak in Hokkien/Teochew) is a beloved Singaporean and Southern Chinese hawker dish consisting of fresh oysters pan-fried together with beaten eggs and a sweet potato starch batter to form a crispy-edged, soft-centred savoury pancake. The dish is typically cooked on a very hot flat iron griddle with lard or oil, giving the edges a characteristic golden crispiness while the centre remains custardy and tender. Spring onions and sometimes bean sprouts are added for freshness. It is served with a tangy-spicy chilli sauce or sambal. The flavour profile is rich, eggy, and briny from the oysters, with a slightly starchy chew from the sweet potato starch batter. Nutritionally, oyster omelette is a moderate-energy dish (~148 kcal per 100 g) providing good-quality protein (7.2 g per 100 g) from both eggs and oysters, along with iron (2.1 mg), zinc (1.8 mg), vitamin B12 (4.5 mcg), and calcium (42 mg). The dish is high in sodium (~380 mg per 100 g) due to fish sauce and seasoning, making it a concern for hypertensive individuals. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low), driven by the sweet potato starch component. Clinically, it is suitable for moderate consumption by people with Type 2 Diabetes due to its low GI, but sodium management is critical for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is not suitable for shellfish-allergic individuals or those avoiding eggs. The dish is a cultural staple at Singapore hawker centres and night markets across Taiwan, Malaysia, and Southern China.
common_user_queries: How many calories in oyster omelette?
Is oyster omelette high in sodium?
What is the GI of oyster omelette?

confidence_reason: HPB Singapore Foodo lists oyster omelette (orh luak) as a hawker dish with nutrient data; values are adapted from this primary regional source. GI is an analogous estimate based on sweet potato starch GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database, as no direct GI measurement for oyster omelette was found in the approved GI literature. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo data for oyster omelette (orh luak), a standard hawker-style preparation. The restaurant version (The Seafood Market, The Riverwalk) at SGD 18.00 likely uses premium oysters and may have slightly higher fat content. 2. GI is an analogous estimate (GI 52) based on sweet potato starch as the primary glycemic driver; no direct GI measurement for oyster omelette was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 3. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source database for this dish. 4. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source database for this dish. 5. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source database for this dish. 6. The dish is not halal-certified in most hawker settings due to the traditional use of lard; some modern versions use vegetable oil and may be halal. 7. Food group has been corrected from the input 'Dairy and eggs' to 'Fish and seafood' as the primary protein and identity of the dish is shellfish (oysters). 8. GL per serving calculation: (GI 52 × carbohydrate_g 11.8 × serving_g 250 / 100) / 100 = 15.34, rounded to 15.3.
